coredata.dc:title: Alcohol dehydration performance of pervaporation composite membranes with reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots homostructured filler
coredata.dc:creator: Lecaros, Rumwald Leo G.
Bismonte, Ma. Elizabeth
Doma, Bonifacio T.
Hung, Wei-Song
Hu, Chien-Chieh
Tsai, Hui-An
Huang, Shu-Hsien
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
Lai, Juin-Yih
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots(GQDs) were used to possibly cover the structural defects on the graphitic regions of reduced graphene oxide(rGO). The resulting homostructure nanocomposite fillers(rGO + GQD) were incorporated in an alginate solution creating a dense composite membrane for pervaporation. The membranes were applied to separate alcohol/water mixtures in which the successful covering of structural defects was observed with good separation performance of lower molecular weight alcohols. The Raman spectra and elemental analyses confirmed the successful changes on the physical and chemical structures of the homostructured fillers. The X-ray diffraction pattern showed that the crystallinity of the membrane was greatly affected when incorporated with rGO + GQD. The alginate incorporated with 3 wt% of rGO + GQD(Alg-rGO + GQD) has the best pervaporation performance in separating methanol/water mixture with a permeation flux of 2323 g m−2 h−1 and water concentration in permeate of 92.7% at 70 °C. While the positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy results supported that the minimization of the structural defects enhanced the water selectivity of the membrane by easy transport of water molecules through the free volume spaces and blocks methanol molecules. The results in this study may provide the possible sealing or curing the defects on rGO with GQDs and improve the interfacial interaction with the polymer matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Alcohol dehydration
Pervaporation
Free volume
Positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of rGO and rGO + GQD
Separation of alcohol/water mixtures by pervaporation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). (a)Visible, UV excitation at 365 nm, and Tyndall effect and (b) TEM image at 200 kV. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of rGO and rGO + GQD. (a) Raman spectra, (b) schematic representation of the proposed stacking of rGO with GQD, and XPS spectrum of (c) rGO and (d) rGO + GQD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM (a,b and d,e) and TEM (c and f) images of (a,b,c) rGO and (d,e,f) rGO + GQD.

Cross-sectional images of composite membranes. (a) Pristine Alg; (b–e) increasing loading of rGO at 0.5, 1,3, and 5 wt%; and (b’-e’) increasing loading of rGO + GQD at 0.5, 1.3, 3, and 5 wt%. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Chemical structure and composition of composite membranes. (a) FTIR spectra and (b) XPS survey of Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD spectra and (b) water contact angle measurement of composite membranes: Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Pervaporation separation performance of (a) Alg-rGO and (b) Alg-rGO + GQD membranes for 70/30 wt% i-PrOH/water mixture at 25 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Pervaporation separation performance of (a) Alg-rGO-3 and (b) Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes for 70/30 wt% alcohol/water mixture at 25 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effect of feed temperature in separating 70/30 wt% MeOH/water mixture using Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate, (b) water and MeOH individual flux, and (c) Arrhenius plots of water and methanol flux. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effect of feed concentration in separating MeOH/water mixture using Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane at 70 °C: (a) Total flux and water concentration in permeate and (b) water and MeOH individual flux. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Solubility parameter of solvent and alginate.
Free volume measurement of Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes under dry-state condition.
Free volume measurement of Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes under wet-state condition.

head.ce:title: Alcohol dehydration performance of pervaporation composite membranes with reduced graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots homostructured filler

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: R.L.G. Lecaros and M.E. Bismonte are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots(GQDs) were used to possibly cover the structural defects on the graphitic regions of reduced graphene oxide(rGO). The resulting homostructure nanocomposite fillers(rGO + GQD) were incorporated in an alginate solution creating a dense composite membrane for pervaporation. The membranes were applied to separate alcohol/water mixtures in which the successful covering of structural defects was observed with good separation performance of lower molecular weight alcohols. The Raman spectra and elemental analyses confirmed the successful changes on the physical and chemical structures of the homostructured fillers. The X-ray diffraction pattern showed that the crystallinity of the membrane was greatly affected when incorporated with rGO + GQD. The alginate incorporated with 3 wt% of rGO + GQD(Alg-rGO + GQD) has the best pervaporation performance in separating methanol/water mixture with a permeation flux of 2323 g m−2 h−1 and water concentration in permeate of 92.7% at 70 °C. While the positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy results supported that the minimization of the structural defects enhanced the water selectivity of the membrane by easy transport of water molecules through the free volume spaces and blocks methanol molecules. The results in this study may provide the possible sealing or curing the defects on rGO with GQDs and improve the interfacial interaction with the polymer matrix.

The successful sealing or curing of structural defects on rGO with GQDs blocks the transport of alcohol molecules and only allow water molecules.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Pervaporation is a membrane-based liquid-liquid separation which is an energy-conservative process in separating azeotropic or close boiling point mixtures. In pervaporation, the mass transfer is widely accepted to follow the solution-diffusion mechanism which comprises of three fundamental steps: (1) sorption of liquid mixture on the surface of the membrane at the feed side; (2) diffusion through the membrane and (3) desorption into the vapour phase at the permeate side. The common polymers used for pervaporation are poly(vinyl alcohol) [1–3], chitosan [4–8] and sodium alginate [9–11] due to their hydrophilic character, ease of process and cost. However, due to their extreme hydrophilicity, these polymers suffer from excessive swelling. Thus, membranologists normally perform cross-linking, blending of polymers or adding inorganic fillers in order to enhance the stability of the membrane. The addition of inorganic fillers such as MOFs [12–14] or GO [8,15–17] is widely studied. One obstacle to face when inorganic fillers are incorporated is the compatibility between then polymer matrix.

Graphene oxide (GO) is currently been the widely studied carbon-based filler due to its hydrophilicity and laminar structure. However, it tends to be unstable for high-water content feed because of the more oxidation groups on the sheets which leads to stronger repulsive hydration force [18]. Also, due to its inherent negative charge it causes electrostatic complexation with the widely used polymers for separation process. The electrostatic complexation could lead to excessive polymer-filler precipitation that is not beneficial for membrane preparation [8]. Thus, the reduction of GO is necessary to stabilize it. During the reduction process, the hydrophilicity of GO would decrease and creates defects on the graphitic region. The use of GO and rGO has been shown to enhance the pervaporation separation performance. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the new emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterial that resembles the structure of GO but confined in small size ranging from 2 to 100 nm [19]. Recent studies of using GOQDs in membrane separation process such as ultrafiltration [20], nanofiltration [21], forward osmosis [22,23] and reverse osmosis [24] have shown good performance when compared with pristine membranes. Since GO and GQDs possess similar structure, the GQDs could potentially “seal” the defects on the graphitic regions of rGO.

Herein, it is reported that the incorporation of GQDs in GO before the hydrothermal reduction process resulted into changes in both physical morphology and chemical structure of rGO. These rGO + GQD fillers enhanced the separation of methanol/water mixtures compare to using rGO. The pervaporation performance of alginate with either rGO or rGO + GQD is evaluated and discussed.

A graphene oxide (GO) suspension was purchased from Angstron Materials (Ohio, USA). Citric acid anhydrous (CA) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets were purchased from Showa chemical industry Co. Ltd (Japan). The alginic acid (sodium salt) was purchased from Acros Organics (New Jersey, USA). The alcohol (methanol, ethanol and isopropanol) were acquired from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd. (Taiwan). The polyethersulfone (PES) support membrane were provided by R&D Center for Membrane Technology (Taiwan).

The GQDs were synthesized as described in our previous study and with slight modifications [1]. Briefly, 2 g of CA was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was put in an oven at 210 °C for 3 h. The successful formation of GQDs was determined by the orange liquefied CA. The orange solution was cooled at room temperature naturally followed by neutralizing it to pH 7.0 with 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH aqueous solution. The resulting dispersion was purified through dialysis using CelluSep H1 dialysis tube (MWCO 2000 Da, Membrane Filtration Products, Inc., Senguin, Texas, USA). The GQDs were kept in refrigerator at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rGO and rGO + GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal reduction of GO was done similarly with our previous study with slight modification [8]. Briefly, a certain amount of 2 wt% GO suspension was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and put in an oven at 90 °C for 72 h. The resulting black suspension was vacuum dried to remove excess water and moisture. On the other hand, the rGO + GQD was synthesized similarly with rGO but an equal amount of 2 wt% GQD solution was mixed with GO prior to hydrothermal reduction. The resulting black suspension after 72 h at 90 °C was filtered and freeze-dried to remove excess water and to produce the powdered sample.

The alginate was dissolved in deionized water to make a 1.5 wt% solution. The fillers, rGO or rGO + GQD, were added into the alginate solution at a varying concentration (0.5, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 wt%) based on the amount of alginate used. For the casting solutions with fillers, the suspension was first sonicated for 30 min followed by homogenizing it at 8000 rpm for 30 min the homogeneous solutions were cast on an air-dried PES support using a 400 μm gap casting knife. The membranes were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h then cooled at room temperature before cross-linking. Then, the membranes were cross-linked in a 0.5 M CaCl2 solution for 10 min and rinsed with methanol and water to multiple times to remove excess CaCl2. The cross-linked membranes were dried in a vacuum oven at room temperature for overnight. The pristine alginate membrane was named as Alg while membranes with either rGO or rGO + GQD was named Alg-rGO-n and Alg-rGO + GQD-n, respectively. The n denotes the concentration of the filler based on the amount of alginate. A homogeneous free-standing membranes in petri dish were also prepared at similar drying and cross-linking conditions for characterization.

The changes in physical and chemical properties of both fillers and membranes were done through attenuated total reflectance - Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) (Spectrum One, Perkin Elmer), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Model S-4800 SEM, Hitachi). While the fillers were further analyzed with Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20 S-TWIN) to determine the successful doping of GQDs onto rGO. The water contact angle (WCA) (Model PV-DP, Face Kyowa Interface Science) was measured to indicate the composite membrane surface hydrophilicity. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Model D8 Advance Eco, Bruker) was used to determine the 2θ of the composite membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of alcohol/water mixtures by pervaporation
ce:section.ce:para: The pervaporation performance of the composite membranes was determined in dehydrating 70 wt% if isopropanol, ethanol and methanol at 25 °C. The composite Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes was used to determine the effect of varying feed concentration and temperatures for methanol/water mixtures. The pervaporation process was performed as described previously [16,17].

The total permeation flux (J, g m−2 h−1) and pervaporation separation factor (α
PV ) were calculated based on the following equations:
(1)
J =
W
A × t
(2)
α
P V
=
Y
W a t e r
Y
a l c o h o l
X
W a t e r
X
a l c o h o l
where W (g) is the weight of the collected permeate at sampling time interval t (h). Y and X refers to the mass fraction of each component in permeate and feed, respectively.

The free volume information of the homogeneous dense membranes was analyzed through positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The pristine Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 were prepared for measurement and analysis as done in previous studies [25–27]. The membranes were pre-soaked in 70/30 wt% MeOH/water mixture for the wet-state condition. The free-volume radius (R
x ) is calculated using equation (2) correlating with the positron lifetime (τ
x ), where x denotes either 3 or 4:
(3)
τ
x
=
1
2
[
1 −
R
x
R
x
+ Δ R
+
(
1
2 π
)
s i n
(
2 π
R
x
R
x
+ Δ R
)
]
− 1
where τ
x is in ns and R
x in Å, and ΔR is equal to 1.656 Å.

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are currently known to have a similar structure and composition to that of graphene oxide (GO). They only differ with the particle size and dimension. The particle size of GQDs ranges to lower than 20 nm while 2-dimensional lateral size of GO could reach up to several micrometers. The GQDs could be synthesized easier and disperse easier than GO, which could be an advantage for exploring new carbon-based nanomaterials for separation processes. The GQDs were synthesized through the “bottom-up” approach which involves the partial pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) at 210 °C. The simple test to determine the successful synthesis of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 . Quantum dots possess a luminescent character when excited using a light at a certain wavelength and are considered to have a dot-like structure if the particles are smaller than 20 nm. Altogether, this was observed with the partial pyrolysis of CA [1,19].

The colloidal solution of the synthesized GQDs emits a blue luminescence under UV light excitation and the formation of a homogeneous colloidal solution was proven by the Tyndall effect. While the TEM images also revealed the uniform distribution of the particles with a size ranging from 3 to 5 nm. The synthesized GQDs will be used to form a homostructured nanofillers by doping it on reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The rGO is known to develop structural defects during the reduction process [28,29]. Reducing of GO is commonly performed to enhance the stability of GO nanosheets especially in membrane separation process that involves high water content [30–32]. These structural defects may also bring non-selective pores along the graphitic region that could negatively affect the membrane selectivity. Thus, in order to maintain the stability of nanosheets in high water concentrated environment, the rGO is proposed to be doped with the GQDs to seal or cure the structural defects.

The GQDs were incorporated in the GO suspension before the hydrothermal reduction process. During the mixing process, the GQDs could stack and interact with the GO sheets through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding. Since the physical structure and chemical composition of GQDs are similar to that of GO. In addition, it could easily intercalate in between the GO sheets and may possibly cover the defects formed during the reduction process.

To characterize or determine the possible changes in the structure of rGO both Raman spectroscopy and XPS analysis was performed as shown in Fig. 2 . The Raman spectra (Fig. 2a) shows the changes in the peak intensities of both the D- and G-band. The D-band at ∼1320 cm−1 represents the sp 3 carbon structure of GO which corresponds to the defects or disorderliness caused by changes in the functional group and covalent bonding. On the other hand, the G-band at ∼1580 cm−1 represents the sp 2 graphitic character of GO. The pristine GO has an I D/I G ratio of 0.73 and it was increased to 1.05 for rGO after the hydrothermal reduction. While the I D/I G ratio of GQD was 0.95. The increase on the D-band indicated the formation of structural defects from the smaller sheets formed and holes along the graphitic region. The doping process showed a decrease in the I D/I G ratio from 1.05 to 0.90 but higher than the pristine GO. This indicated that the rGO + GQD, which was prepared with a 1:1 wt ratio of GO and GQD, undergone reduction since the G-band increased due to the stacking of GQDs along the graphitic regions or edges of rGO as proposed in Fig. 2b.

The XPS spectra revealed a number of peaks that corresponds to the characteristic functional groups of GO. These four types of carbon bonds are: C–C (284.4 eV), C–O (286.0 eV), CO (283.3 eV), and O–CO (289.4 eV). The C–C bond in which the rGO + GQD particles resulted to a slightly higher peak area percentage of 54.4% compared to rGO with 53.7%. This small increase could be caused by the stacking of GQDs onto the graphitic regions of rGO. Besides, the presence of oxygen functional groups is still beneficial for the hydrophilicity of the particles.

Further changes on the properties of rGO after doping with GQD was observed through SEM (Fig. 3 ) and TEM images of the particles. From the SEM images, the surface morphology of the particles has obvious changes. The rGO revealed a typical GO sheet structure with smooth and wrinkled surface while the rGO + GQD showed a rougher and corrugated surface. For the TEM images, the particles were exfoliated in water in order to observe the physical structure of the sheets. Similar to what was found in SEM, the rGO showed smoother sheets than rGO + GQD. The rGO + GQD sheets also appeared to be larger and have more ripples.

The rGO and rGO + GQD were integrated in alginate to make a composite pervaporation membrane using PES as a supporting membrane. The changes on the cross-sectional morphology was observed through SEM images shown in Fig. 4 . The pristine Alg membrane has a thickness of 325 ± 25 nm. The hybrid membrane, either integrated with rGO or rGO + GQD, resulted to form a thicker selective layer which may come from the expansion brought by the stacks of rGO or rGO + GQD sheets along the alginate matrix. The series of Alg-rGO + GQD membranes also appeared to have thicker selective layers compared to their Alg-rGO membranes counterpart. The thickness of the selective layer could affect the separation performance due to mass transfer resistance. In addition, for all membranes, especially for the hybrid membranes, there were no observable defects and the interface between the PES support and the alginate is clear. This could also prove that the molecular interaction between alginate and the particle filler is good resulting into a dense and thin selective layer that is efficient for alcohol dehydration through pervaporation.

The chemical structure and composition of the composite membranes Alg, Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 is shown in Fig. 5 . The FTIR spectra of the membranes (Fig. 5a) revealed an observable change in the absorbance peaks intensity and shift. The pristine Alg membrane revealed the highest absorbance at all characteristic peaks especially for the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups at ∼3400 cm−1 and ∼1718 cm−1, respectively. Upon integrating the particle fillers, the absorbance surprisingly decreased and shifted to a lower wavenumber from ∼3400 cm−1 to ∼3200 cm−1 which could be caused by the changes in the orientation of the alginate polymer chain and its hydrogen bonding. The characteristic peaks of alginate remained at the same position. The Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane has shown to have higher absorbance peak intensities as compared to Alg-rGO-3 membrane which may indicate that the former has stronger hydrogen bonding. The abundant hydroxyl groups present in GQD was responsible for the enhancement of the hydroxyl peak and hydrogen bonding (Fig. S1). On the other hand, the elemental survey analysis through XPS is shown in Fig. 5b the C/O ratio increased when either rGO or rGO + GQD was integrated into the alginate. The Alg membrane has a C/O ratio of 1.27 and was increased to 1.54 when rGO was integrated. The C/O ratio for the Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane further increased to 1.87. As listed in Table S1, the C/O ratio of GO is 1.79 which was increased to 2.24 after reduction (rGO) due to carbonization and removal of oxygen functional groups. While the GQD also possess a high carbon percentage its C/O ratio was 2.86 which made the rGO + GQD particle to have a higher C/O ratio of 3.49. The increased atomic carbon percentage in the membrane may come from the addition of the carbon-based particle fillers.

The crystallinity of the polymer matrix and hydrophilicity of the composite membranes was further investigated using XRD analysis in and water contact angle (WCA) measurement in Fig. 6 . The diffraction peak of Alg membrane was observed at around 2θ = 13.3°. The peak height declined after the integration of rGO or rGO + GQD which may indicate that more amorphous regions are created. The creation of amorphous regions is beneficial for the mass transport of permeants. The enhancement of the amorphous regions was caused by the hydrogen bond effect where the particles act as barriers for the alginate polymer chains to form hydrogen bond to another alginate polymer chain [1,9,33,34]. This disturbance in hydrogen bonding rearranges the packing of the polymer chains. In addition, the addition of the particle fillers may enlarge the interchain distance that could also improve the mass transport. The characteristic peak of GO or rGO at 2θ ≈ 10° when added in polymer could not be observed which is a result of good dispersion within the polymer matrix.

The composite membranes were tested in separating 70/30 wt% isopropanol/water (i-PrOH/water) mixture at 25 °C as shown in Fig. 7 . Since the kinetic diameter of i-PrOH (4.7 Å) is larger than water (3.0 Å), the differences in the total flux and water selectivity of the membrane could be easily observed. The rGO loaded membranes (Fig. 7a) showed an increase in both water flux and water concentration in permeate compared with pristine Alg membrane. The increase in total flux from 1520 ± 19 g m−2 h−1 to 2020 ± 81 g m−2 h−1 was caused by the possible presence of water channels that was brought about by the aligned stacking of rGO sheets. The increased number of interlayers rendered a more tortuous path that further enhanced the water selectivity by blocking the i-PrOH molecules [9]. The longer tortuous pathway would let the permeating molecules to be transported slowly due to additional mass transfer resistance. During the permeation, larger molecules such as the alcohol will be more restricted. Hence, smaller sized permeants like water can easily be transported. The separation factor dropped from 23331 to 2544 after 3 wt% loading. Here, the constructed water channels may have collapsed due to the possible agglomeration of rGO nanosheets. However, below 3 wt% loading, the flux was slightly higher which may also come from the formed amorphous regions. In addition, the fast water transport along the graphitic regions of rGO was favorable to the improved flux. The flux increased at the highest loading (5 wt%) which is due to the agglomerated rGO sheets.

The Alg-rGO + GQD membranes appeared to have a similar trend with rGO (Fig. 7b). However, it resulted into a lower flux but with comparable separation factor. The lower flux from these particle fillers could prove the possible sealing or curing of structural defects. Since, both water and i-PrOH molecules could be transported into the non-selective defects in the Alg-rGO membranes but with GQDs they are blocked. The diffusion rate difference between water and alcohol is high showing improved membrane water selectivity. The performance of Alg-rGO and Alg-rGO + GQD at 3 wt% loading is comparable and outstanding. This also show that the structural defects on rGO is good enough for separating i-PrOH and water molecules. However, it would be interesting to observe that the possible sealing or curing of these defects by using QDs could potentially enhance the separation of smaller alcohols such as methanol and ethanol.

The separation of methanol or ethanol from water is far more challenging due to their molecular size, higher interaction with water and prone to membrane swelling. The Alg-rGO-3 and Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes were used to separate 70/30 wt% alcohol/water mixture at 25 °C as shown in Fig. 8 .

Surprisingly, both MeOH/water and EtOH/water flux is lower compared to i-PrOH/water for all types of membrane, even with the pristine Alg. This is an unusual behaviour observed for alginate membranes and overlooked [35–37]. In other polymeric membranes, the smaller the alcohol molecular size would result to a higher flux. This indicates that the alginate membranes have high water selectivity due to good hydrophilicity and solubility property with water. The solubility parameter of the permeant and polymer is presented in Table 1 to explain why does the flux in MeOH/water and EtOH/water mixture is lower than i-PrOH/water mixture.

Herein, the interaction between the permeant-permeant (solvent-solvent) and permeant-polymer (solvent-alginate) is considered. Based from the solubility parameter values, the lower the difference between the values would mean higher interaction. MeOH and EtOH forms stronger interaction with water with a solubility parameter difference of 18.20 MPa1/2 and 21.29 MPa1/2, respectively, than i-PrOH with water with 24.23 MPa1/2. This shows that the interaction between i-PrOH molecules and water are easier to break. On the other hand, water and alginate have the strongest interaction among the permeants with 13.67 MPa1/2. Which means the polymer matrix that is comprised mostly of alginate chains would attract the water molecules more than the alcohol. However, considering the interaction between alcohol and water, the alginate and alcohol are competing interacting with water. Thus, with MeOH and EtOH the pulling of water molecules is high thus decreasing the transport of it through the membrane. Hence, lower flux for smaller alcohol/water mixture. In i-PrOH/water mixture the water is easily absorbed by alginate because of stronger interaction of water molecules with alginate.

It is noteworthy to mention that the enhancement of separation factor could obviously been seen especially for smaller alcohols (Fig. 8b). The Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane has the highest separation factor compared with Alg-rGO-3 membrane. Again, this proved the sealing or curing the defects on rGO by forming a homostructure morphology which could be beneficial for separating smaller molecular size alcohols from water.

The separation of methanol and water is far more challenging since the only available membranes that could separate this mixture is made from inorganic materials, which are costly. The polymeric membranes, on the other hand, suffers from excessive swelling from methanol and water thus the separation efficiency is low. Therefore, the effect of pervaporation operating conditions for MeOH/water mixture (i.e. water concentration in feed and feed temperature) was evaluated using Alg-rGO + GQD-3. The effect of the feed temperature is separating 70/30 wt% MeOH/water mixture is shown in Fig. 9 .

The flux and water concentration in permeate rapidly increased due to the changes in the vapour pressure in the feed while the permeate side was maintained under vacuum conditions, thus an increase in the driving force enhanced the flux. Another reason for the increase of flux from 655 ± 14 g m−2 h−1 at 25 °C to 2323 ± 45 g m−2 h−1 at 70 °C was due to the rapid changes in the mobility of both permeant and polymer chain with increasing temperature. Interestingly, the water concentration in permeate also increased from 87 ± 3% at 25 °C to 92.7 ± 0.03% 70 °C with temperature showing the breaking of the “trade-off” phenomenon. The enhanced water selectivity could be explained by the changes in the individual fluxes of the permeant as shown in Fig. 9b. It can be observed that the flux of water is way higher when compared to MeOH. Which means that the membrane selects and transports water molecules while blocking and slowing down the transport methanol molecules. A similar observation was found for the commercial membrane, PERVAP 2201 by Sulzer Chemtech, where the membrane selectivity increased with feed temperature [39]. Therefore, the transport of water molecules through the membrane was easier at higher temperature. In order to show the temperature dependency of the flux with temperature, an Arrhenius plot is shown in Fig. 9c. The relationship of feed temperature and individual permeation flux (J
i ) of each component i is determined through the Arrhenius equation given:
(4)
J
i
= A
e
−
E
a
R T
where A is the pre-exponential factor, E
a is the apparent activation energy of component i; R is the ideal gas constant and T is the operating feed temperature. The E a was obtained by plotting ln(J
i ) vs 1000/T.

The apparent activation energy (E
a ) of water is 23.7 kJ/mol and 12.7 kJ/mol for methanol. The E
a,water is greater than E
aMeOH which means that the increase rate of water flux was higher than MeOH flux. It also suggests that water responds with the temperature increase more than MeOH. Thus also explaining the enhanced water selectivity of the membrane.

The water concentration in feed was varied and done at 70 °C because the membranes could maintain the water content in permeate at high concentration (∼92.7%). Fig. 10 shows the effect of water content in feed on the pervaporation separation performance of Alg-rGO + GQD-3. The total flux increased with increasing water content in feed. The individual fluxes of water and MeOH simultaneously increased with water content in feed. The plasticizing effect caused this increase in flux due to the high hydrophilic character of the membrane and it was more evident at water content in feed of 70 and 90 wt%. The membrane swells at high water content creating larger free volume spaces that transports both water and methanol molecules through the membrane. Interestingly, the water concentration in permeate also increased with temperature. The high water content in the permeate was due to the faster diffusion of water than methanol through the membrane. The increased water flux was brought by the increased water partial pressure due to high concentration of water in feed. However, the MeOH flux also increases with water content in feed due to the plasticizing effect but the rGO + GQD could have prevented or blocked the methanol molecules and would be transported into a long and tortuous path. Altogether, the successful sealing or curing of structural defects of rGO was proved from characterization to the pervaporation performance in separating MeOH/water mixtures.

The fine-structure of the alginate membranes with or without the fillers under dry and wet conditions was analyzed through positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS). The free volume information of the membranes is shown in Table 2 for the dry-state condition and in Table 3 for the wet-state condition. The o-Ps lifetime, τ3 or τ4, is related to how long does the positron stay within the free volume space. Which means, the longer the lifetime is the larger the free volume. The τ3 could be related to the free volume size (R 3) and intensity (I 3) of the polymer chains. On the other hand, τ4 can also be translated into R 4 or I 4 and represents the free volume information of the interface between the polymer and the filler and also between polymer aggregates [40] which was also observed in other types of mixed matrix membranes [41,42].

It can be observed that the free volume size under dry-state conditions are smaller compared to the wet-state conditions. This shows that when the membrane contacts the feed mixture the polymer chain would form larger free volume. Hence, the appearance of τ4. It could also be observed that the R 3 under wet-state condition became smaller than the measured values in the dry-state. This happened due to the coalescence of smaller free volume making more and larger spaces. It is noteworthy that in either dry- or wet-state, the free volume size for Alg-rGO + GQD appeared to be smaller than Alg-rGO. The smaller free volume size could be correlated to the minimization of the structural defects in rGO. The free volume intensity of Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membranes have the highest value among the membranes which could be another reason for the enhancement of the water selectivity of the membrane. Overall, the resulting free volume size under wet-state condition proved that the Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane has better alcohol blocking capability since the free volume size is smaller than the kinetic diameter of methanol (3.8 Å) but large enough for the transport of water (2.6 Å).

In addition, a long-term stability test was performed in separating 70/30 wt% MeOH/water mixture at 70 °C for 240 h (Fig. S2). The total flux was maintained at ∼2000 g m−2 h−1 and a water concentration in permeate of ∼93% for 240 h, showing good stability and separation performance.

Reduce graphene oxide (rGO) is known to form structural defects during the reduction process. This uncontrolled effect could create non-selective pores along the graphitic region and makes the separation efficiency of the membrane to suffer. Thus, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were shown to possibly seal or cure the structural defects of rGO. The Raman spectra showed the transformation of GO to rGO and into the rGO + GQD where changes in the I D/I G was observed. The physical morphology, observed under SEM and TEM, of the rGO + GQD was found to gain changes compared to rGO. The homostructured rGO + GQD presented a rougher and more wrinkled morphology than rGO. In order to show the sealing or curing of the structural defects, the synthesized homostructured rGO + GQD particle fillers were integrated in alginate to fabricate a dense composite membrane for the pervaporation separation of i-PrOH/water mixture. The Alg-rGO + GQD membranes showed lower flux than Alg-rGO membranes which proved that the structural defects of rGO contributed to the fast transport of the permeating molecules. However, the separation of i-PrOH/water mixture was exceptional that the effect of sealing the defects could not be properly observed. Hence, the separation of smaller alcohol such as methanol and ethanol was performed. The separation of smaller alcohol/water mixture show better separation factor for membrane integrated with rGO + GQD since the permeating molecules could not be transported through the structural defects of rGO. The composite membrane, Alg-rGO + GQD-3, has the best pervaporation performance in separating methanol/water mixture with a permeation flux of 2323 ± 45 g m−2 h−1 and water concentration in permeate of 92.7 ± 0.03% at 70 °C. The free volume size information given by the positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) also supported that the minimization of the structural defects enhanced the water selectivity of the membrane by allowing the transport of water molecules (2.6 Å) through the free volume spaces (3.109 Å) and blocks methanol molecules (3.8 Å). In addition, the pervaporation separation performance of the Alg-rGO + GQD-3 membrane was stable when tested for 240 h. The results in this study provided the possible sealing or curing the defects on rGO with GQDs and could be beneficial for the separation of smaller alcohol/water mixture.

Rumwald Leo G. Lecaros: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Ma. Elizabeth Bismonte: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Bonifacio T. Doma: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Wei-Song Hung: Investigation, Resources, Supervision. Chien-Chieh Hu: Supervision. Hui-An Tsai: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shu-Hsien Huang: Supervision. Kueir-Rarn Lee: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Juin-Yih Lai: Supervision.
